title: शिव मङ्गलाष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिव मङ्गलाष्टकम् ॥

भवाय चन्द्रचूडाय नृत्ताय गणशालिने।
शिवाय शङ्कराय च मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥१॥

शिवाय गौरीनाथाय चन्द्रार्धकृतशेखराय।
भवाय भवनाशाय मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥२॥

नीलकण्ठाय वृषभध्वजाय शशिमौलिने।
तस्मै नकाराय विद्महे मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥३॥

पञ्चवक्त्राय भीमाय त्रिनेत्राय च शूलिने।
शिवाय शान्तरूपाय मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥४॥

गङ्गाधराय सोमाय अर्धनारीश्वराय च।
पशुपतये नमस्तुभ्यं मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥५॥

कपालिने भैरवाय मृत्युञ्जयाय शम्भवे।
व्योमकेशाय रुद्राय मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥६॥

वामदेवाय महते ईशानाय नमो नमः।
सर्वेश्वराय सर्वाय मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥७॥

कैलासशिखरे रम्ये शङ्करस्य मनोहरे।
सदा वसेत् शिवं सत्यं मङ्गलं शम्भवे नमः॥८॥

मङ्गलाष्टकमेतद्यो मङ्गलं शम्भवे पठेत्।
सर्वार्थसिद्धिमाप्नोति शिवलोके महीयते॥

॥ इति श्री शिव मङ्गलाष्टकं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shiva Mangalashtakam ||

Bhavaya Chandrachoodaya Nrittaya Ganashaline.
Shivaya Shankaraya Cha Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||1||

Shivaya Gaurinathaya Chandrardhakritashekharaya.
Bhavaya Bhavanashaya Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||2||

Neelakanthaya Vrishabhadhvajaya Shashimauline.
Tasmai Nakaraya Vidmahe Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||3||

Panchavaktraya Bheemaya Trinetraya Cha Shooline.
Shivaya Shantaroopaya Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||4||

Gangadharaya Somaya Ardhanareeshvaraya Cha.
Pashupataye Namastubhyam Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||5||

Kapaline Bhairavaya Mrityunjayaya Shambhave.
Vyomakeshaya Rudraya Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||6||

Vamadevaya Mahate Ishanaya Namo Namah.
Sarveshvaraya Sarvaya Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||7||

Kailasashikhare Ramye Shankarasya Manohare.
Sada Vaset Shivam Satyam Mangalam Shambhave Namah.||8||

Mangalashtakam-Etadyo Mangalam Shambhave Pathet.
Sarvarthasiddhim-Apnoti Shivaloke Maheeyate.

|| Iti Shri Shiva Mangalashtakam Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
१. भव, चन्द्रचूड, नृत्यप्रिय, गणों के स्वामी शिव शंकर को मंगल नमस्कार।

२. गौरीनाथ, अर्धचन्द्रधारी, भवनाशक शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

३. नीलकण्ठ, वृषभध्वज, शशिमौलि शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

४. पञ्चमुखी, भीम, त्रिनेत्रधारी, शूलपाणि, शान्तस्वरूप शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

५. गंगाधर, सोम, अर्धनारीश्वर, पशुपति शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

६. कपाली, भैरव, मृत्युञ्जय, व्योमकेश, रुद्र शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

७. वामदेव, महेश्वर, ईशान, सर्वेश्वर शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

८. कैलास के रमणीय शिखर पर विराजमान सत्यस्वरूप शिव शम्भु को मंगल नमस्कार।

जो इस मंगलाष्टक का पाठ करता है, उसे सर्वार्थसिद्धि प्राप्त होती है और वह शिवलोक में सम्मानित होता है।
benefits: Invokes the auspicious blessings of Lord Shiva in eight verses
Ideal for beginning any new venture or auspicious occasion
Traditionally invoked to establish a protective auspicious aura around the devotee
Powerful recitation during Maha Shivaratri and Mondays
Invokes the blessing of peace, prosperity, and divine grace
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Shiva Mangalashtakam (शिव मङ्गलाष्टकम्) stands as an eight-verse devotional hymn. Rooted in Shiva Purana, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to create a protective auspicious aura around the devotee as well as bestow peace, prosperity, and divine grace. Additionally, it is known to invoke the auspicious blessings of Lord Shiva in eight verses. This sacred text is ideally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan month for enhanced blessings. At 8 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 5 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Mangalashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shiva Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Mangalashtakam (शिव मङ्गलाष्टकम्) is an eight-verse mangala-stotra in praise of Lord Shiva, drawn from the Shiva Purana stotra tradition. The mangalashtakam genre — auspicious eight-verse prayers — is recited at the start of new ventures, at weddings, on festival mornings and during major sankalpas to invoke divine blessings. Each verse of the Shiva Mangalashtakam concludes with the refrain Mangalam Shambhave Namah — 'salutations and auspiciousness to Shambhu (Shiva).' The hymn invokes Shiva through his most beloved epithets: Bhava, Chandrachuda, Shankara, Gauri-natha, Nilakantha, Vrishabhadhvaja, Panchavaktra, Trinetra, Shulin, Gangadhara, Soma, Ardhanarishvara, Pashupati, Kapali, Bhairava, Mrityunjaya, Vamadeva, Mahesha, Ishana, Sarveshvara. (Source: Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the refrain 'Mangalam Shambhave Namah' mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The refrain Mangalam Shambhave Namah concludes every verse of the Shiva Mangalashtakam. Mangalam — 'auspiciousness, blessing'; Shambhave — 'to Shambhu' (in the dative case — Shambhu is Shiva as the giver of bliss); Namah — 'salutations.' Together: 'Salutations and auspiciousness to Shambhu.' Like all mangala-vachana refrains, the structure invokes blessings upon the Lord himself, by which the chanter in turn becomes the recipient of divine grace. The choice of Shambhu (the giver of sham — peace and bliss) is theologically significant — the chanter is invoking specifically the bliss-bestowing aspect of Shiva, not the destructive Rudra aspect. (Source: Shiva Mangalashtakam refrain in verses 1-8.)

seoFaqs.question: Which forms and aspects of Shiva are praised across the eight verses?
seoFaqs.answer: The eight verses systematically traverse Shiva's complete iconography. V1 — Bhava (Existence), Chandrachuda (Moon-crested), Nritta (Cosmic Dancer), Ganashalin (Lord of the ganas), Shankara. V2 — Gauri-natha (consort of Gauri), Chandrardha-kritashekhara (half-moon-crowned), Bhava-nashana (destroyer of samsara). V3 — Nilakantha (blue-throated from drinking Halahala), Vrishabhadhvaja (whose banner is the bull), Shashimaulin (moon-diademed). V4 — Panchavaktra (five-faced), Bhima (terrible), Trinetra (three-eyed), Shulin (trident-bearer), Shanta-rupa (peaceful form). V5 — Gangadhara (Ganga-bearer), Soma (with Uma), Ardhanarishvara (half-male-half-female), Pashupati (lord of all beings). V6 — Kapalin (skull-bearer), Bhairava, Mrityunjaya (conqueror of death), Vyomakesha (sky-haired), Rudra. V7 — Vamadeva, Mahadeva, Ishana, Sarveshvara. V8 — Kailasashikhara-vasi (dweller on Kailash), Satya. (Source: Shiva Mangalashtakam verses 1-8.)

seoFaqs.question: When and on what occasions should the Shiva Mangalashtakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Mangalashtakam is most powerful on Monday (Somvar — Shiva's day), and is especially auspicious on Maha Shivaratri (Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi — the Great Night of Shiva), throughout the entire month of Shravan / Sawan (when Shiva-puja peaks across India), on every Shravan Somvar, on Pradosha vrata (every 13th tithi of both pakshas), on Sankashti Chaturthi, on Nag Panchami, on Sawan Shivaratri, on Mahashivaratri-eve and morning, on Shiva Trayodashi, during the Kavad Yatra, and at the opening of every new venture, business inauguration, griha-pravesha, marriage ceremony and Shiva-puja sankalpa. The chanter sits facing north or east, applies bhasma and rudraksha, lights ghee lamps, offers bilva leaves, and chants the eight verses with full bhava. (Source: Shiva Purana paddhati; Maha Shivaratri tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting the Shiva Mangalashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti declares: Mangalashtakam-Etadyo Mangalam Shambhave Pathet, Sarvarthasiddhim-Apnoti Shivaloke Maheeyate — 'one who recites this Mangalashtakam dedicated to Shambhu attains the fulfilment of all aims (sarvartha-siddhi) and is honoured in Shivaloka.' Traditional benefits include: invocation of auspiciousness for new beginnings (business openings, weddings, griha-pravesha, journeys); creation of a protective auspicious aura around the devotee; sarvartha-siddhi (success in dharma, artha, kama and moksha); peace, prosperity and divine grace; protection from akala-mrityu (untimely death) through Mrityunjaya and Bhairava aspects; healing through the Mrityunjaya and Vyomakesha epithets; awakening of the Ardhanarishvara balance of Shiva-Shakti within; and ultimately Shivaloka-prapti (eternal residence in Kailash with Shiva-Parvati). (Source: Shiva Mangalashtakam phalashruti.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Shiva Mangalashtakam differ from the Shiva Ashtakam and Lingashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: These three Shiva-stotras serve distinct ritual functions. The Shiva Mangalashtakam is a mangala-vachana stotra (8 verses) whose specific purpose is invoking auspiciousness — recited at ritual beginnings, with the refrain Mangalam Shambhave Namah at every verse. The Shiva Ashtakam (multiple traditions exist, e.g. Adi Shankara's 'Prabhum Prananatham Vibhum Vishvanatham') is primarily a darshana-stotra that paints meditative imagery of Shiva's form for contemplation. The Lingashtakam ('Brahma Murari Surarchita Lingam') is a worship-hymn focused specifically on the Shiva-linga form, recited during abhisheka and puja of the linga. Devotees often recite all three on Maha Shivaratri and during Shravan Somvars: the Mangalashtakam at the start, the Ashtakam during darshana, and the Lingashtakam during abhisheka. (Source: classical Shiva stotra-paddhati.)
